params.text:
Duty of care, safeguarding and whistleblowing

Your duty of care is closely linked to safeguarding — protecting people from abuse, neglect and harm. If your duty of care makes you suspect that someone is being harmed, or is at risk of harm, you have a responsibility to act on that concern and report it. Doing nothing is not an option.

Whistleblowing means raising concerns about unsafe, unethical or illegal practice within your organisation — for example, poor care, a colleague working unsafely, or attempts to cover up mistakes. The law protects workers who raise genuine concerns in good faith.

Both safeguarding and whistleblowing flow directly from your duty of care: keeping people safe sometimes means speaking up, even when it feels uncomfortable.

Speaking up the right way

If you have a concern:

  • report it to your line manager or the person named in your safeguarding policy without delay
  • if the concern is about your manager, use your organisation's escalation route
  • if you feel your concern is not being acted on, you can raise it externally — for example with the CQC or the local safeguarding team
  • record what you saw or heard, sticking to the facts

You do not need to be certain that abuse is happening to raise a concern. It is not your job to investigate — it is your job to pass on what worries you so that the right people can look into it.

params.question: You suspect a colleague is not caring for someone safely, but you are not completely sure. What should you do?

answers.text: Report your concern to your manager or the named safeguarding lead, sticking to the facts

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. You do not need to be certain. Raising a genuine concern so the right people can look into it is exactly what your duty of care requires.

answers.text: Say nothing until you are 100% sure something is wrong

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Waiting for certainty can leave someone at risk. You should report a genuine concern even when you are not sure.

answers.text: Investigate it yourself and confront the colleague

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: It is not your role to investigate. Report the concern so trained people can look into it properly and safely.

answers.text: Discuss it with other staff but keep it off the record

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Gossip does not protect the person. Concerns must be reported through the proper route and recorded accurately.
